intro:
First Peter 3:18-20 states that Christ, 'being put to death in the flesh but made alive in the Spirit,' in the Spirit 'went and made proclamation to the spirits in prison who disobeyed long ago when God waited patiently in the days of Noah while the ark was being built.' This verse is among the most disputed in the NT. The 'spirits in prison' has been interpreted as: (1) deceased human sinners who perished in the flood (requiring post-mortem proclamation); (2) fallen angels or spirits identified with the 'sons of God' of Genesis 6 (imprisoned since the flood — cf. Jude 6; 2 Pet. 2:4), to whom Christ proclaimed his victory; or (3) OT human dead to whom Christ preached during the interval between his death and resurrection.

ISBE reviews the major positions. The angelic interpretation (2) is supported by the term pneumata (spirits) without 'dead' or 'human' qualification — in NT usage bare 'spirits' more often refers to supernatural beings — and by the explicit connection to Noah's time and the Genesis 6 background evident in 2 Peter 2:4-5 and Jude 6. On this view, Christ's proclamation is not evangelism offering salvation but a declaration of victory over the imprisoned beings who corrupted the pre-flood world (cf. Col. 2:15: 'having disarmed the powers and authorities, he made a public spectacle of them, triumphing over them by the cross'). Whatever the precise interpretation, the passage affirms Christ's cosmic lordship extending into the realm of the imprisoned dead, and his resurrection as the definitive victory over all hostile powers, concluded in the summary of 1 Peter 3:22: 'angels, authorities and powers in submission to him.'
